title: Fine-Tuning & PEFT
description: Fine-tuning adapts a pre-trained LLM to a specific task or style. FAANG ML interviews test your understanding of full fine-tuning vs parameter-efficient methods (LoRA, QLoRA), when to fine-tune vs RAG, and the compute/memory math.

sections.title: Full Fine-Tuning vs PEFT
sections.description:
Full fine-tuning updates all model weights. For a 7B model at FP16, that's 14GB of weights + ~3× for optimizer states (Adam) = ~56GB VRAM minimum. Impractical without a GPU cluster.

PEFT (Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning) freezes most weights and only trains a small adapter — achieving 90%+ of full fine-tuning quality with 1–2% of the parameters.

MethodTrainable ParamsVRAM (7B)Quality
Full FT (FP16)100%~56 GBBest
LoRA (r=16)~0.1–1%~20 GBNear-best
QLoRA (INT4)~0.1–1%~6–8 GBNear-LoRA
Prefix Tuning<0.1%~14 GBGood
sections.codeBlock:
# VRAM calculation for fine-tuning
def finetuning_vram(params, dtype_bytes=2):
    weights = params * dtype_bytes          # FP16
    gradients = params * dtype_bytes        # same as weights
    # Adam optimizer: 2 states (momentum + variance) in FP32
    optimizer = params * 4 * 2
    return weights + gradients + optimizer  # bytes

params_7b = 7e9
total = finetuning_vram(params_7b)
print(f'Full FT 7B: {total/1e9:.0f} GB VRAM')
# → 56 GB — needs 2× A100 80GB

# LoRA reduces optimizer states to only adapter params
def lora_vram(params, lora_params_frac=0.001, dtype_bytes=2):
    frozen_weights = params * dtype_bytes   # weights still loaded
    lora_p = params * lora_params_frac
    grad_opt = lora_p * (dtype_bytes + 4 * 2)  # grad + adam states
    return frozen_weights + grad_opt

print(f'LoRA 7B:   {lora_vram(params_7b)/1e9:.0f} GB VRAM')
# → ~16 GB — fits on 1× A100 40GB

sections.title: LoRA: Low-Rank Adaptation — The Math
sections.description:
LoRA (Hu et al., 2021) is the most widely used PEFT method. The key insight: weight updates during fine-tuning lie in a low-dimensional subspace. Instead of updating W (large), train two small matrices A and B such that ΔW = BA, where B ∈ ℝ^{d×r} and A ∈ ℝ^{r×k}, with rank r ≪ min(d,k).

Trainable params: r×(d+k) instead of d×k. For a 4096×4096 attention weight with r=16: 16×8192 = 131,072 vs 16,777,216 — 128× fewer parameters.

Alpha (α): Scaling factor. Effective ΔW = (α/r) × BA. Setting α=2r keeps the scale stable regardless of r. This is a hyperparameter — tune alongside r.
sections.codeBlock:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

class LoRALinear(nn.Module):
    """Wraps a frozen Linear layer with LoRA adapter."""
    def __init__(self, linear: nn.Linear, rank: int, alpha: float):
        super().__init__()
        self.linear = linear
        self.rank = rank
        self.scale = alpha / rank   # scaling factor

        d_out, d_in = linear.weight.shape
        # A initialized with Gaussian, B initialized with zeros
        # → ΔW = BA = 0 at start → no change to model initially
        self.A = nn.Parameter(torch.randn(rank, d_in) * 0.01)
        self.B = nn.Parameter(torch.zeros(d_out, rank))

        # Freeze original weights
        for p in self.linear.parameters():
            p.requires_grad = False

    def forward(self, x):
        # Original path + low-rank adaptation
        return self.linear(x) + self.scale * (x @ self.A.T @ self.B.T)

# Param count comparison
d = k = 4096; r = 16
full_params = d * k                    # 16,777,216
lora_params = r * (d + k)              # 131,072
print(f'Reduction: {full_params/lora_params:.0f}x fewer trainable params')

sections.title: QLoRA — Quantized LoRA
sections.description:
QLoRA (Dettmers et al., 2023) combines 4-bit quantization of the frozen base model with LoRA adapters trained in BF16. Key innovations:

NF4 (NormalFloat4): A 4-bit data type that's information-theoretically optimal for normally distributed weights (which LLM weights are, post-training). Better than INT4 for this distribution.
Double Quantization: Quantize the quantization constants themselves — saves ~0.5 bits/parameter additional.
Paged Optimizers: Use NVIDIA unified memory to page optimizer states from GPU to CPU when VRAM is full — prevents OOM during long context training.

Result: 65B model fine-tuned on a single A100 80GB. This democratized LLM fine-tuning.
sections.codeBlock:
# QLoRA setup with Hugging Face + bitsandbytes
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, BitsAndBytesConfig
from peft import get_peft_model, LoraConfig, TaskType
import torch

# Step 1: Load model in 4-bit (NF4)
bnb_config = BitsAndBytesConfig(
    load_in_4bit=True,
    bnb_4bit_quant_type='nf4',          # NormalFloat4
    bnb_4bit_compute_dtype=torch.bfloat16,  # compute in BF16
    bnb_4bit_use_double_quant=True,     # double quantization
)

model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
    'meta-llama/Llama-3-8B',
    quantization_config=bnb_config,
    device_map='auto',
)

# Step 2: Add LoRA adapters on top of 4-bit frozen base
lora_config = LoraConfig(
    r=16,                # rank
    lora_alpha=32,       # alpha = 2 * rank
    target_modules=['q_proj', 'k_proj', 'v_proj', 'o_proj'],  # attention weights
    lora_dropout=0.05,
    bias='none',
    task_type=TaskType.CAUSAL_LM,
)

model = get_peft_model(model, lora_config)
model.print_trainable_parameters()
# trainable params: 20,480,000 || all params: 8,051,343,360 || 0.25%

sections.title: When to Fine-Tune vs RAG vs Few-Shot
sections.description:
This is a system design decision question at FAANG. The right answer depends on what you're changing about the model.

GoalBest ApproachWhy
Answer from private docsRAGGrounded, updatable, no retraining
Domain-specific format/styleFine-Tune (SFT)Model learns output format, tone
New task with few examplesFew-shot promptingNo training cost, fast iteration
Reduce hallucination on factsRAGFine-tuning doesn't reliably inject facts
Safety / alignmentRLHF / DPORequires preference optimization
Private domain + styleRAG + Fine-TuneBest of both — most production systems

Key insight: Fine-tuning teaches the model how to behave. RAG gives it what to say. They solve different problems.
sections.codeBlock:
# SFT data format (instruction tuning)
# The model learns to follow this template after seeing 10K+ examples
import json

def create_sft_example(instruction, response):
    return {
        'messages': [
            {'role': 'system',
             'content': 'You are an expert SQL assistant.'},
            {'role': 'user',
             'content': instruction},
            {'role': 'assistant',
             'content': response}
        ]
    }

# Example SFT dataset entry
example = create_sft_example(
    instruction='Write a SQL query to find the top 5 customers by revenue.',
    response=(
        'SELECT customer_id, SUM(amount) as revenue\n'
        'FROM orders\n'
        'GROUP BY customer_id\n'
        'ORDER BY revenue DESC\n'
        'LIMIT 5;'
    )
)

# Data quality > data quantity for SFT
# 1K high-quality pairs often beats 100K noisy ones (Alpagasus paper)
print(json.dumps(example, indent=2))

sections.title: Evaluation: Benchmarks & LMSYS
sections.description:
How do you know if your fine-tuned model is actually better? Use both automated benchmarks and human evaluation.

Automated benchmarks: MMLU (knowledge), HellaSwag (commonsense), HumanEval (code), GSM8K (math), MT-Bench (instruction following). Run with lm-evaluation-harness (EleutherAI).
LLM-as-judge: Use GPT-4 to score model outputs on criteria (accuracy, coherence, relevance) 1–10. Correlates well with human preference (Chatbot Arena).
Task-specific evals: For production, always evaluate on a held-out test set from your actual task distribution — generic benchmarks are a poor proxy.

sections.title: References & Further Reading
sections.description:
• LoRA: Low-Rank Adaptation of Large Language Models (Hu et al., 2021) — The most influential PEFT method, standard at FAANG for efficient fine-tuning
  • QLoRA: Efficient Finetuning of Quantized Language Models (Dettmers et al., 2023) — LoRA + 4-bit NF4 quantization, fine-tune 65B on a single GPU
  • Prefix-Tuning: Optimizing Continuous Prompts for Generation (Li & Liang, 2021) — Lightweight alternative to full fine-tuning
  • HuggingFace PEFT Library — Implementation of all major PEFT methods (LoRA, Prefix, IA3, AdaLoRA)
  • HuggingFace TRL — Transformer Reinforcement Learning — SFTTrainer, DPOTrainer for fine-tuning with RLHF support

details:
Catastrophic Forgetting: Fine-tuning on a narrow task causes the model to lose its general capabilities. Mitigations: (1) Include a mix of general instruction data with your task data (~5–20% general), (2) Use PEFT (LoRA) — adapters don't modify the base weights, preserving general knowledge, (3) Use elastic weight consolidation (EWC) for continual learning scenarios.

LoRA Rank Selection: r=8 is a good default for simple style transfer. r=16–32 for complex task adaptation. r=64–128 for tasks very different from pre-training. Higher rank = more expressivity but more memory and risk of overfitting. α=2r is the standard starting point. Use W&B sweeps to find optimal r for your dataset.

Merging LoRA Adapters: After training, LoRA weights (BA) can be merged back into the base model: W_merged = W + (α/r)×BA. This adds zero inference latency. Multiple adapters can be merged with task arithmetic or DARE-TIES for multi-task models.
